text: Dr. Siegel here showing a robotic ventral hernia repair with a preperitoneal tap technique. This is my preferred technique for simple peri-umbilical and ventral defects.
The surgeon identifies a dimple indicating the hernia and begins establishing the correct plane for the peritoneal flap, entering the pre-peritoneal space.
The surgeon develops the space inferiorly, avoiding the retro-rectus space, with visible fat and a potential arcuate line in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues to develop the preperitoneal space, ensuring that the mesh placement remains outside the abdominal cavity, avoiding contact with the bowels.
The surgeon continues to work superiorly, maintaining the integrity of the preperitoneal plane while leaving the posterior layer of the rectus sheath intact and unviolated.
The surgeon uses sharp dissection and electrocautery to further develop the preperitoneal space, maintaining the integrity of the surrounding tissue layers.
The surgeon identifies the hernia defect, characterized by the volcano sign, as the hernia protrudes into the preperitoneal space.

The surgeon has developed the preperitoneal space both above and below the hernia area. The video shows the removal of fat from a small port site hernia, with the characteristic volcano sign of the hernia visible.
The surgeon begins addressing the hernia and hernia sac, revealing preperitoneal fat within the hernia defect as dissection progresses.

The surgeon carefully removes preperitoneal hernia fat from the defect, ensuring it descends back into the abdominal cavity while maintaining the integrity of the preperitoneal space and keeping the peritoneal flap intact.

The surgeon identifies a piece of fat stuck in the developed space and proceeds to close the hernia defect using a locking V-lock suture. The suture technique involves taking multiple bites of fascia above the defect and imbricating the hernia sac to close the dead space, aiming to reduce seroma formation.
The surgeon leaves the suture slightly loose and sequentially tightens it, visibly adjusting the tension along the suture line as the procedure progresses.

The surgeon closes the main defect using a suture, then moves inferiorly to address a smaller port site defect. The suture is used to take healthy bites of fascia as the surgeon approaches the tiny port site hernia.
The surgeon takes two bites on either side of the hernia defect to close it securely, ensuring the fascia is properly approximated.
The surgeon sequentially tightens the suture, ensuring the closure of the defect while maintaining tension across the previously placed stitches.
Sometimes I drop the pneumo peritoneum pressure. This defect came together pretty easily.
The surgeon completes the suturing by backing the V-lock suture on itself to secure the closure. After taking a couple of additional bites, the suture is cut, finalizing the closure of the defect.
The surgeon places a piece of permanent synthetic mesh into the pre-peritoneal space, ensuring wide overlap of the mesh over the defect area.

The surgeon uses Vicryl interrupted sutures to fixate the synthetic mesh in the pre-peritoneal space, securing the mesh at its four corners to ensure it remains tented out and flat, preventing any wrinkling or curling.

The surgeon fixates the mesh inferiorly using a single suture for efficiency, ensuring the mesh is securely in place. The flap is then closed with a V-lock suture and absorbable trio, completing the closure process.
The surgeon demonstrates the pre-peritoneal tap technique, positioning the mesh outside the abdominal cavity to avoid exposure to the bowel, achieving a wide mesh overlap.

minimally invasive trans-abdominal approaches as opposed to open. Sometimes it's a little more challenging, especially in overweight patients, to get that meshed preperitoneal without making a larger incision.

The surgeon utilizes a three-port technique with three eight-millimeter incisions to perform the procedure. The flap is closed to exclude the mesh from the abdominal cavity, and the surgeon runs the suture back on itself to lock the flap, completing the final product.
The surgeon closes the preperitoneal space with the mesh positioned outside the abdominal cavity, completing the repair.
